title: Dog Diarrhea with Mucus: Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps
meta_desc: Is your dog's stool slimy or jelly-like? Learn why dogs have mucus in their diarrhea, when it's an emergency, and how to help your pup today.
content_html:
TL;DR: Mucus in your dog's stool typically indicates colon irritation (colitis) and is usually not an emergency unless your dog is also vomiting, lethargic, or showing significant blood.

What does it mean if my dog has diarrhea with mucus?

Finding a jelly-like coating or slimy substance in your dog's stool can be startling, but it is a very common clinical sign. Mucus is a normal substance produced by the lining of the large intestine to keep the colon lubricated. When the colon becomes irritated or inflamed—a condition known as colitis—it overproduces this mucus as a protective response. This results in stool that looks like it is encased in a glossy film or clear-to-white jelly.

When is mucus in my dog's stool considered an emergency?

  • The urgency level for mucus in diarrhea is generally Low if your dog is still energetic and drinking water.
  • If your dog hasn't vomited, this is likely a mild case of intestinal upset that can be managed with monitoring and a bland diet.
  • Contact a veterinarian urgently if the diarrhea is accompanied by extreme lethargy, uncontrollable vomiting, or large amounts of bright red blood.

How does taking a photo of my dog's stool help with veterinary triage?

  • Taking a clear photo of the stool is one of the most helpful things you can do for a triage team, as descriptions like "slimy" can be subjective.
  • A photo allows a professional to see the exact color and the ratio of mucus to solid waste.
  • Visual evidence helps identify whether any blood or parasites are visible.
  • This helps the team determine if your dog needs to be seen immediately or if home care is appropriate.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, large-bowel diarrhea is characterized by frequent defecation with decreased fecal volume, urgency, tenesmus, and mucus in the feces, often accompanied by fresh, red blood (hematochezia). While weight loss is uncommon, it may be present if the small intestine is also involved. Mucosal damage, which can cause secretory and permeability diarrhea, may result from inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), enteric pathogens (viruses, pathogenic bacteria, Giardia, Histoplasma, Pythium), dietary sensitivity, acute radiation disease (ARD), or intestinal neoplasia such as lymphosarcoma. A thorough rectal examination is warranted to rule out rectal polyps or malignant neoplasms.

Chapter: Gastroenterology, Infectious Disease, Diagnostic Imaging, Laboratory Medicine

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 157)

faqs.question: What causes mucus in a dog's stool?
faqs.answer: Common causes include dietary indiscretion (eating something they shouldn't), stress, intestinal parasites like giardia, or sudden changes in their regular food.

faqs.question: Should I fast my dog if they have diarrhea with mucus?
faqs.answer: A short fast of 12 hours can help rest the digestive tract, followed by a bland diet of boiled chicken and white rice, but always ensure they have plenty of fresh water.

faqs.question: When should I call the vet immediately?
faqs.answer: Contact your vet if the diarrhea persists for more than 48 hours, if your dog is a young puppy, or if they show signs of pain, dehydration, or weakness.
